quote: the mainly just the prices of the electric bill recently as well as it's just easy to provide for myself and not have to worry about things like that.
stewart_read: Yovanni handed the rep a two-part motivation — bill pain and a self-sufficiency value — within the first 45 seconds. The rep acknowledged it ('No. You're okay.') and moved immediately to the map tool. The bill anchor was never deepened, and the self-sufficiency angle was never touched again. This is the emotional core of the pitch, and it was captured and dropped.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may have been intentionally deferring the bill anchor to complete address verification first, planning to return after confirming the property. This hypothesis is falsified by the call's subsequent trajectory: after the map tool failed at 01:05, the rep never returned to qualification content of any kind. No bill amount was asked, no emotional flip was attempted, and the call ended at 02:49 without revisiting Yovanni's stated motivation. The deferral was not strategic — it was abandoned.
coaching_implication: Show Tyke this moment in the 1-on-1. The customer gave him the emotional anchor on a platter. Ask: 'What would you have done with that if the map had worked?' Then ask: 'Why couldn't you do that same thing while the map was broken?' The bill anchor does not require satellite imagery.

quote: Let me get a better view of it. It's not automatically pinning the home.
stewart_read: The rep hit a satellite imagery tool failure and had no verbal pivot. The correct move was to acknowledge the tool issue briefly, then continue the verify sequence verbally — utility company, bill amount, roof age, title/ownership — while the design team resolved the imagery. Instead, the rep spent the next 90 seconds collaborating with Yovanni on property identification, which is design-team work, not setter work. No codex section directly covers satellite imagery tool failure during address verification — this is a protocol gap for Kenny to address.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may have needed the confirmed address before proceeding with qualification. Grounded counter: Yovanni confirmed the address at 00:28 ('Yes, sir.') — the address was already confirmed. The imagery failure was a design-tool problem, not an address-confirmation problem. The rep conflated the two.
coaching_implication: This is a tool-dependency gap. Tyke needs a verbal pivot protocol: when the map tool fails, say something like 'Our design team will sort the imagery — let me keep moving on my end so we don't waste your time' and continue the verify sequence. Manager should role-play this scenario explicitly.

quote: It's a single family, trailer home.
stewart_read: Yovanni identified the home as a single-family trailer home. The rep did not follow up to confirm ownership — verify.address_homeowner_confirmed requires ownership confirmation, not just home type. Whether manufactured/mobile homes are eligible for Ion's program is not addressed in the codex's dq_rules section and should be treated as a Kenny/TBD item, not a disqualifier. What IS grounded: the rep never asked 'And you own the home?' — a required verify item that was skipped.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may have inferred ownership from the fact that Yovanni submitted a solar inquiry. This is insufficient — renters occasionally submit solar inquiries, and the codex marks homeowner confirmation as hard-required with a hard DQ for non-owners.
coaching_implication: Flag the ownership gap in the 1-on-1. The rep confirmed home type but not ownership. On a trailer home specifically, the ownership question is more important, not less — land ownership vs home ownership distinctions can affect eligibility. Manager should also surface the manufactured-home eligibility question to Kenny for a definitive answer.

quote: You'll see about, like, three buildings on that property
stewart_read: Yovanni mentioned three buildings on the property. This is a potential secondary-structure signal — detached structures (garages, workshops, barns) can represent additional solar or roofing opportunity. The rep was focused on property identification and did not register this as a cross-sell signal. The signal is soft — Yovanni was describing the property layout, not asking about secondary structures — but it warranted a mental note and potentially a brief probe.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep was appropriately focused on confirming the correct structure for the primary solar install and did not have bandwidth for cross-sell probing during a tool-failure recovery. This is a reasonable read — the cross-sell miss here is lower-severity than a direct customer statement about needing a new roof. Still worth flagging as a pattern to build.
coaching_implication: Low-priority coaching item for this call, but worth building the habit: when a customer mentions multiple structures, note it. A single question — 'Are any of those other buildings yours as well?' — takes five seconds and can surface a secondary-structure opportunity. Manager can use this as a brief add-on in the 1-on-1 after addressing the primary gaps.

quote: So let me just talk to them, and then I can give you a callback here a little bit later.
stewart_read: The rep exited with an unlocked, unscheduled callback — 'a little bit later' has no date, no time, and no mutual commitment. Per button_up, a locked time is required for any follow-up to have structural integrity. On a paid lead (where the bill is required before the appointment can be set), an unlocked callback is a likely miss. The rep also gave no indication of what Yovanni should prepare for the callback, which reduces the probability of a productive reconnect.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may have judged that locking a specific time was premature given the unresolved imagery issue — better to call back once the design team has confirmed the property. This is partially reasonable, but it does not preclude locking a time: 'Let me get this sorted with my team — can I call you back tomorrow at [specific time]?' costs nothing and dramatically improves callback conversion.
coaching_implication: This is the most immediately coachable moment on the call. Show Tyke the difference between 'I'll call you back later' and 'Can I reach you tomorrow at 2pm?' The locked time is the only thing that separates a real callback from a cold re-dial. Manager should drill this as a standalone habit: every exit, even a tool-failure exit, ends with a locked time.

quote: I'll keep it short for you. My job here at Ion is just to put together this design to see if it's a good fit for the home. Just have a couple of quick questions for you.
stewart_read: This is a competent, low-pressure opener. The rep framed his role accurately (design fit, not sales pitch), set a time expectation ('keep it short'), and moved efficiently to qualification. This is the strongest moment on the call — the rep's instincts at the open were correct. It makes the subsequent tool-failure collapse more instructive: the rep had the right frame at the start and lost the thread when the environment changed.
coaching_implication: Acknowledge this explicitly in the 1-on-1. The open was clean. The coaching conversation should start here: 'You had the right frame at the top — what happened when the map didn't load?' This positions the feedback as building on a strength, not cataloguing failures.

quote: Bought where I saw it or where on Instagram. Sorry.
stewart_read: Yovanni began to add a third motivation — something he saw on Instagram — then self-interrupted and apologized. The rep responded with 'No. You're okay.' and moved on. This was a second enthusiasm signal in the same breath as the first: the customer was actively trying to share more context about his interest. The rep's response was not dismissive in tone, but it did not invite Yovanni to complete the thought. A brief 'No, go ahead — what did you see?' would have deepened the emotional anchor and surfaced additional motivation the rep could have used later.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may have judged that the bill-pain motivation was sufficient and that pursuing the Instagram thread would extend the call unnecessarily. Given that the call ended at under three minutes with no qualification completed, call-length economy was not the binding constraint here.
coaching_implication: Pair this with the 00:41 moment in the 1-on-1. Yovanni tried to give the rep two separate emotional anchors in the first minute. The rep collected neither. Ask Tyke: 'What would you have done with the Instagram comment if you'd followed it?' The answer builds the habit of treating customer asides as data, not interruptions.

stewart_read: By 01:47, the rep has spent over 40 seconds collaborating with Yovanni on satellite imagery navigation — describing roof colors, door colors, and building positions. This is design-team work, not setter work. The rep's scope drifted from qualification to property identification assistance, which is both outside the setter's role and a poor use of the customer's time. The customer remained cooperative, but the call's productive window was being consumed by a task the rep cannot complete.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may have been trying to maintain rapport by staying engaged with Yovanni's descriptions rather than going silent while checking the tool. This is a reasonable instinct, but the correct move was to redirect: 'I've got enough to flag for my design team — let me keep moving on the qualification side while they sort the imagery.'
coaching_implication: Use this as the scope-creep illustration for this call. The rep was doing design-team work on a setter call. The coaching question: 'What's your job when the map doesn't load?' The answer should be: continue qualification verbally, flag the imagery issue to the design team, and don't spend the customer's time on a problem you can't solve.
